attributes.stem:
An 83-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with three days of worsening mid-back pain. The pain is severe, constant, and worse with movement. He has metastatic prostate cancer diagnosed two years ago with known bone metastases. His medical history includes chronic kidney disease stage 3, hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and osteoarthritis. His medications include goserelin, abiraterone, prednisolone, apixaban, bisoprolol, amlodipine, and regular paracetamol. He lives independently and mobilises with a walking stick.

On examination, he appears uncomfortable. His pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 138/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. There is marked tenderness over the T8 vertebra. He has no neurological deficits in his lower limbs, with normal power, sensation, and reflexes. His bladder and bowel function are normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms and signs which may suggest a pathological vertebral fracture secondary to metastatic prostate cancer. The absence of neurological deficits suggests that there is no spinal cord compression, making this a stable pathological fracture requiring symptomatic management in the first instance.

Paracetamol and oxycodone is the most appropriate initial management. Adequate analgesia is essential to allow mobilisation and prevent complications of immobility. A combination of regular simple analgesia with breakthrough opioids provides good pain control while maintaining alertness. In chronic kidney disease, opioids should be used cautiously with reduced doses and careful monitoring.

Urgent MRI spine would be indicated if there were signs of spinal cord compression (neurological deficits, bladder/bowel dysfunction) or if the pain was associated with radiculopathy. In this case, MRI can be arranged on a non-urgent basis to assess the extent of metastatic disease.

Urgent radiotherapy referral may be considered for pain management if the patient does not respond to conventional analgesia, but it is not the first-line treatment for uncomplicated pathological fractures.

Vertebroplasty referral might be considered later if conservative management fails, but it is not an emergency procedure and carries risks in the setting of metastatic disease.

Physiotherapy alone would be inappropriate as initial management without adequate analgesia first. While physiotherapy and early mobilisation are important aspects of care, the severe pain needs to be controlled before rehabilitation can commence effectively. There is also a risk of further fracture if mobilisation is attempted without proper pain control.

The patient should be monitored closely for the development of neurological symptoms or signs which would necessitate urgent imaging and specialist referral. The underlying cancer treatment should also be reviewed by the oncology team.
learningPoints: Pathological vertebral fractures in metastatic cancer require urgent MRI only if neurological deficits are present.
Initial management of uncomplicated pathological vertebral fractures focuses on adequate analgesia and early mobilisation.
Regular assessment for neurological deterioration is essential in patients with spinal metastases.
NSAIDs and high-dose opioids should be avoided in chronic kidney disease due to risk of further renal impairment.
references: Mansoorinasab M, Abdolhoseinpour H. A review and update of vertebral fractures due to metastatic tumors of various sites to the spine: Percutaneous vertebroplasty. Interv Med Appl Sci. 2018;10(1):1-6. doi:10.1556/1646.10.2018.03. Available online at ; https://akjournals.com/view/journals/1646/10/1/article-p1.xml

Cho JH, Ha JK, Hwang CJ, Lee DH, Lee CS. Patterns of treatment for metastatic pathological fractures of the spine: the efficacy of each treatment modality. Clinics in Orthopedic Surgery. 2015 Dec 1;7(4):476-82. Available online at ; https://synapse.koreamed.org/articles/1050360#B3
